title: STANDARD FOR PROCESSED CEREAL-BASED FOODS FOR INFANTS AND YOUNG CHILDREN

sections.section: STANDARD FOR PROCESSED CEREAL-BASED FOODS FOR INFANTS AND YOUNG CHILDREN
sections.summary: Document title only: Standard for Processed Cereal‑Based Foods for Infants and Young Children.
Standard identifier and history: CXS 74-1981, adopted 1981, revised 2006, and amended in 2017, 2019 and 2023.

Notes 2023 amendments which aligned food additive provisions with the General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, CXS 192-1995) and made changes to Section 3 (Essential composition and quality factors).

Covers processed cereal‑based foods intended as complementary foods generally from about 6 months of age and for young children as part of a progressively diversified diet, in line with relevant WHO/WH Assembly guidance.

Processed cereal‑based foods must be prepared primarily from one or more milled cereals constituting at least 25% of the final mixture on a dry weight basis, and the standard distinguishes four product categories (see Sections 2.1.1–2.1.4) and definitions for age groups in Section 2.2.

Defines four product categories: 2.1.1 cereals to be prepared with milk/nutritious liquids; 2.1.2 cereals with added high‑protein to be prepared with water/protein‑free liquids; 2.1.3 pasta to be cooked in boiling water; and 2.1.4 rusks and biscuits to be used directly or after pulverization with liquids.
Defines 'infant' as a person up to 12 months of age and 'young children' as persons older than 12 months up to 36 months (3 years).

Sets essential composition and quality requirements including primary cereal ingredients, minimum energy density, protein quality and quantity rules, limits on added sugars and lipids (with fatty acid specifications), mineral and vitamin minima/ranges, permitted optional ingredients and processing/quality practices, and explicitly prohibits treatment by ionizing radiation and the use of partially hydrogenated fats.

Requires products to be prepared primarily from milled cereals (e.g., wheat, rice, maize, millet, sorghum) and allows legumes, starchy roots or oil seeds in smaller proportions, and states that energy and nutrient requirements refer to the product ready for use as marketed or prepared per manufacturer instructions.

sections.section: 3.1.2 The requirements concerning energy and nutrients refer to the product ready for use as marketed or prepared according to the instructions of the manufacturer, unless otherwise specified.
sections.summary: Specifies that the energy and nutrient requirements apply to the product ready for use, either as marketed or prepared according to manufacturer instructions, unless otherwise stated.
The energy density of processed cereal‑based foods should not be less than 3.3 kJ/g (0.8 kcal/g).

Specifies protein quality and quantitative limits: added protein must have a chemical index ≥80% of casein or PER ≥70% of casein (with amino acids allowed only to improve protein value and only as necessary, using natural L‑forms), and sets maximum and minimum protein content limits for specified product categories (see 3.3.2–3.3.4).

sections.section: 3.3.1 The chemical index of the added protein shall be equal to at least 80 percent of that of the reference protein casein or the protein efficiency ratio (PER) of the protein in the mixture shall be equal to at least 70 percent of that of the reference protein casein.  In all cases, the addition of amino acids is permitted solely for the purpose of improving the nutritional value of the protein mixture, and only in the proportions necessary for that purpose. Only natural forms of L-amino acids should be used.
sections.summary: Requires added protein to have a chemical index of at least 80% of casein or a PER of at least 70% of casein, and permits addition of amino acids only to improve protein nutritional value and only in necessary proportions using natural L‑amino acids.

sections.section: 3.3.2 For  products  mentioned  in  Sections 2.1.2  and  2.1.4,  the  protein  content  shall  not  exceed  1.3 g/100 kJ (5.5 g/100 kcal).
sections.summary: Sets a maximum protein content for products in Sections 2.1.2 and 2.1.4 of 1.3 g/100 kJ (5.5 g/100 kcal).

sections.section: 3.3.3 For  products  mentioned  in  Section 2.1.2  the  added  protein  content  shall  not  be  less  than  0.48 g/100 kJ (2 g/100 kcal).
sections.summary: Requires that products in Section 2.1.2 contain at least 0.48 g added protein per 100 kJ (2 g/100 kcal).

sections.section: 3.3.4 For biscuits mentioned in Section 2.1.4 made with the addition of a high protein food, and presented as such, the added protein shall not be less than 0.36 g/100 kJ (1.5 g/100 kcal).
sections.summary: For biscuits in Section 2.1.4 presented as containing an added high-protein food, the added protein must be at least 0.36 g/100 kJ (1.5 g/100 kcal).

Limits added simple carbohydrates for complementary/processed cereal foods: for products in Sections 2.1.1 and 2.1.4 added sucrose/fructose/glucose/glucose syrup/honey ≤1.8 g/100 kJ (7.5 g/100 kcal) with fructose ≤0.9 g/100 kJ (3.75 g/100 kcal), and for products in Section 2.1.2 those limits are ≤1.2 g/100 kJ (5 g/100 kcal) and fructose ≤0.6 g/100 kJ (2.5 g/100 kcal).

sections.section: 3.4.1 If sucrose, fructose, glucose, glucose syrup or honey are added to products mentioned in Sections 2.1.1 and 2.1.4:
sections.summary: If sucrose, fructose, glucose, glucose syrup or honey are added to products in Sections 2.1.1 and 2.1.4, added carbohydrates from these sources shall not exceed 1.8 g/100 kJ (7.5 g/100 kcal) and added fructose shall not exceed 0.9 g/100 kJ (3.75 g/100 kcal).

sections.section: 3.4.2 If sucrose, fructose, glucose, glucose syrup or honey are added to products mentioned in Section 2.1.2
sections.summary: If sucrose, fructose, glucose, glucose syrup or honey are added to products in Section 2.1.2, added carbohydrates from these sources shall not exceed 1.2 g/100 kJ (5 g/100 kcal) and added fructose shall not exceed 0.6 g/100 kJ (2.5 g/100 kcal).

When prepared according to label directions, processed cereal-based foods must have a texture appropriate for spoon-feeding the intended infant/young child age, and rusks/biscuits may be used dry to encourage chewing or reconstituted with liquid to a consistency similar to dry cereals.
Prohibits treating the product or its components with ionizing radiation and prohibits the use of partially hydrogenated fats in these products.

Permits specified functional additives (acidity regulators, anticaking agents, antioxidants, carriers, emulsifiers, packaging gases, raising agents and thickeners) when used per Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192‑1995) for food category 13.2. Only additives listed for category 13.2 may occur by carry‑over, subject to (a) amounts in raw materials not exceeding specified maxima and (b) the final food not containing more additive than would be introduced under good manufacturing practice, consistent with the General Standard's carry‑over provisions.

sections.section: 4.1 Acidity regulators, anticaking agents, antioxidants, carriers, emulsifiers, packaging gases, raising agents and thickeners used in accordance with Table 1 and Table 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 1921995) 1  in food category 13.2 (Complementary foods for infants and young children) are acceptable for use in foods conforming to this standard.
sections.summary: Authorizes use of specified functional additives (acidity regulators, anticaking agents, antioxidants, carriers, emulsifiers, packaging gases, raising agents and thickeners) in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of CXS 192‑1995 for food category 13.2 (complementary foods for infants and young children).

sections.section:
4.2 Only the food additives listed in food category 13.2 (Complementary foods for infants and young children) of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) 1  may be present in the foods conforming to this standard, as a result of carry-over from a raw material or other ingredient (including food additive) used to produce the food, subject to the following conditions:
- a) the amount of the food additive in the raw materials or other ingredients (including food additives) does not exceed the maximum level specified; and
- b) the  food  into  which  the  food  additive  is  carried  over  does  not  contain  the  food  additive  in  greater quantity  than  would  be  introduced  by  the  use  of  the  raw  materials  or  ingredients  under  good manufacturing practice, consistent with the provisions on carry-over in the preamble of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995). 1
sections.summary: Allows only additives listed for food category 13.2 to be present via carry‑over from ingredients, provided (a) the additive amount in raw materials/ingredients does not exceed specified maxima and (b) the final food does not contain more of the additive than would be introduced by use of those raw materials/ingredients under good manufacturing practice.
Specifies permitted flavourings and maximum use levels: natural fruit extracts and vanilla extract at good manufacturing practice levels, and ethyl vanillin and vanillin each up to 70 mg/kg.

Mandates preparing products with special care under good manufacturing practices to minimize pesticide residues appropriate to the product and target population, and requires the product to be free from residues of hormones and antibiotics (as determined by agreed methods) and practically free from other contaminants, especially pharmacologically active substances.

Requires products to be prepared with special care under good manufacturing practices so that pesticide residues do not remain or, if technically unavoidable, are reduced to the maximum extent possible.

Requires products to be free from residues of hormones and antibiotics (detectable by agreed analytical methods) and to be practically free from other contaminants, particularly pharmacologically active substances.

Recommends preparing and handling products in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969) and relevant Codex codes of practice, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21‑1997.

Requires packaging that safeguards the hygienic and other qualities of the food and that containers/packaging materials be made only of substances safe and suitable for intended use, complying with applicable Codex standards where established.

sections.section: 7.1 The product shall be packed in containers which will safeguard the hygienic and other qualities of the food.
sections.summary: States that the product shall be packed in containers which will safeguard the hygienic and other qualities of the food.

Applies the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods, Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling and Guidelines for Use of Nutrition and Health Claims to these foods (with possible national restrictions on pictorial devices); permits nutrition claims under national law only if demonstrated by rigorous scientific studies; and requires labelling information to be provided in the appropriate language(s) of the country of sale.

Requires the food name to indicate the true nature of the product (e.g., "Dry cereal for infants (and/or young children)", "Rusks for infants (and/or young children)", "Biscuits (or 'milk biscuits') for infants (and/or young children)", or "Pasta for infants (and/or young children)") or use an appropriate designation in accordance with national legislation.

Requires a complete list of ingredients on the label in descending order of proportion, except vitamins and minerals may be grouped separately (and need not be in descending order within those groups), and mandates declaration of the specific name for ingredients and food additives while allowing inclusion of appropriate class names.

Mandates nutrition declaration showing energy (kcal and kJ) and amounts of protein, carbohydrate and fat per 100 g or 100 ml (and per specified quantity/serving where appropriate), the average amounts of specified vitamins and minerals defined in Sections 3.6 and 3.7 per 100 g/100 ml, any other nutrition information required by national law, and allows voluntary declaration of other vitamins and minerals not covered in 8.4.1(b).

Requires the date of minimum durability preceded by 'best before' (day/month/year in uncoded numerical sequence, or month/year where shelf-life >3 months, with 'end (year)' permitted in specified cases), obliges indication of any special storage conditions if the date depends on them, and where practicable storage instructions should be placed close to the date marking.

Requires labels or accompanying leaflets to provide directions for preparation, use and storage before and after opening; for products covered by Section 2.1.1 labels must state 'Milk or formula but no water shall be used for dilution or mixing' (or equivalent), a 'gluten-free' statement may be used when composed of gluten-free ingredients/additives, and labels must state the recommended minimum age for use (not less than 6 months) plus advice to consult a health worker about the precise timing of complementary feeding, with possible additional national requirements.

sections.section: 8.6.2 For products covered by Section 2.1.1, directions on the label shall state 'Milk or formula but no water shall be used for dilution or mixing' or an equivalent statement.
sections.summary: For products covered by Section 2.1.1, label directions must state 'Milk or formula but no water shall be used for dilution or mixing' or an equivalent statement.

sections.section: 8.6.3 When the product is composed of gluten-free ingredients and food additives, the label may show the statement 'gluten-free'. i
sections.summary: Permits the label statement 'gluten-free' when the product is composed of gluten-free ingredients and food additives.

sections.section: 8.6.4 The label shall indicate clearly from which age the product is recommended for use. This age shall not be less than 6 months for any product. In addition, the label shall include a statement indicating that the decision when precisely to begin complementary feeding, including any exception to 6 months of age, should be made in consultation with a health worker, based on the individual infant's specific growth and development needs. Additional requirements in this respect may be made in accordance with the legislation of the country in which the product is sold.
sections.summary: Requires the label to state clearly the recommended age for use (not less than 6 months) and to include a statement that the exact timing of complementary feeding, including any exception to 6 months, should be decided in consultation with a health worker based on the infant's individual needs, with possible additional national requirements.
Prohibits presenting the products covered by this standard as breast-milk substitutes; they are explicitly not breast-milk substitutes.
